The six-figure job-hopping era in tech is over. Goldman Sachs warns displaced engineers now face six-month searches and pay cuts of up to 30%. This isn't a blip—it's a structural shift.

Over 260,000 tech jobs were cut in 2023. Another 165,000 in 2022. The market hasn't bounced back. Mid-career professionals are hit hardest.

Why? AI-driven productivity. Tools that let a team of ten do the work of three or four. The leverage has shifted from workers to employers.

Companies are adapting:
🔹 Hiring senior engineers in global hubs at a fraction of SF salaries
🔹 Pivoting roles toward AI-adjacent fields like ML engineering and data infrastructure
🔹 Leveraging contract and freelance work over full-time hires

What does this mean for you?
The adjustment period will be long. Re-skilling is no longer optional. Accepting lower compensation or looking outside traditional tech roles might be necessary to stay employed.

This isn't about doom and gloom. It's about clear-eyed adaptation. The sector's revenues are strong, but the labor model is changing.

How are you preparing for this new reality?

#TechJobs #FutureOfWork #AI

The six-month job search is the new normal for many in tech. Goldman Sachs warns the AI shift isn't a blip—it's a structural reset of the entire labor market.

Their analysis points to a sobering reality: over 425,000 tech layoffs in 2022-2023 were just the beginning. The real change is in the aftermath.

What's happening now?

🔹 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗦𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗲𝘀: Median job hunts now stretch six months or longer.
🔹 𝗣𝗮𝘆 𝗖𝘂𝘁𝘀: Compensation is dropping 10-30% for displaced mid-career professionals.
🔹 𝗔𝗜➖𝗗𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗘𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆: Teams of ten are being reduced to three or four through coding assistants and automated tools.

The leverage has shifted from workers to employers. The era of easy six-figure job-hopping appears to be over for many.

This isn't just a Bay Area story. Seattle, Austin, New York, and remote hubs are all feeling the pressure. Companies are leveraging global talent pools, hiring senior engineers abroad at a fraction of previous costs.

So, what's the path forward?

Goldman suggests a prolonged adjustment period. Workers are pivoting to AI-adjacent fields like machine-learning engineering or data infrastructure. Others are turning to contract work.

The sector's financials remain strong, but the labor market is contracting. This creates a difficult tension between corporate performance and individual careers.

This shift demands more than just updating a resume. It requires a fundamental re-skilling and, for some, a redefinition of what a 'tech career' looks like.

Have you seen this shift in your network or company? How are people adapting?

Most founders I know are unknowingly paying $44/hour for deep competitor analysis.

That's the average hourly rate for a due diligence analyst in the US.
And honestly, I was the biggest victim of this.

Not because I was hiring analysts. But because I was doing it myself. Round after round of prompting. Tweaking. Regenerating. Still ending up with surface-level summaries that missed the depth I actually needed. 

What should've taken 30 minutes was eating hours of my day.
Then I found one prompt that changed my entire research process.

Here's what nobody tells you: the research you do before you write a single line of code or before you even think about assembling a team determines everything that comes after. 

Here's the 10-step process of what it covers:

1. What the company does; founding, product, value proposition, how it actually works.
2. Business segments; one deep sub-section per segment
3. Products & business detail; full catalogue, manufacturing, geographies
4. Customers; who buys, why they buy, switching costs, concentration, contract structure
5. Competitive landscape; named competitors, why this company wins or loses, barriers to entry
6. Industry; demand drivers, size, regulation, cyclicality
7. Growth triggers; from earnings calls only, every point cited with concall date
8. Key risks; specific to this company, mechanism explained
9. Walk the talk; management credibility across 4 concalls, promises vs. outcomes
10. Scenarios; bull / base / bear as stories, no numbers, no targets

No complex setup. No coding. Just:
→ Open Claude Cowork
→ Paste the prompt
→ Enter a company name
→ Get a structured, analyst-quality report

I've shared this with other founders, investors and operators doing competitive research. The quality genuinely surprised people who use AI tools every day.

Want the complete guide I use every time I research a company or competitor?
